content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
From 
: 
Subject: 
( 1 ) 
Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
FAIR bPOLITICAL. PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Bar ara Milman 
Administrative Sanctions 
Dote 
~'1ay 21, 1980 
Power of the Commission to impose sanctions other than those 
listed in Section 83116. 
Section 83116 of the Act empowers the Commission, upon finding 
~ violation of the Act, to issue an order requiring the violator 
to: 
(a) 
Cease and desist his violation; 
(b) 
File any reports, statements or other documents or 
information required by the Act; or 
(c) 
Pay a monetary penalty .... 
Section 83116 also provides that the Commission shall have all 
the powers granted by Chapter 5 of the Administrative Procedure 
Act, including Government Code Section 11519. 
Section 11519 
provides: 
(b) 
A stay of execution may be included in the decision 
or if not included therein may be granted by the agency at 
any time before the decision becomes effective. 
The stay of 
execution provided herein may be accompanied by an express 
condition that respondent comply with specified terms of 
probation~ provided, however, that the terms of probation 
shall be just and reasonable in the light of the findings 
and decision. 
Combining the language of the Act with that of Section 11519, the 
Commission is empowered to stay any penalty imposed under the 
Act, and to impose any conditions of probation which are just and 
reasonable as a condition of the stay. 
A case involving almost an identical statutory situation was 
decided in 1977 in Governing Board of the Chaffey Union High 
School District v. Comm'n. on Professional Competence, 72 Cal· 


Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
May 21, 1980 
Page Two 
App. 3d 447 (1977). 
In that case the Commission on Professional 
Competence ordered that a teacher be permanently dismissed, and 
then ordered the dismissal permanently stayed subject to the 
condition that the teacher take a two year leave of absence without 
pay and that he not request attorney's fees. 
The Commission on 
Professional Compentence was acting under a statute which gave it 
authority only to dismiss an employee or to order that the employee 
not be dismissed. 
The Commission was not given any other remedial 
powers by the statute. 
However, the statute did provide that its 
proceedings would be covered by the APA, and that it would have 
"all the power granted to an agency by the APA." 
Thus the statutory 
authority of the Commission on Professional Competence in the 
Chaffey case was almost exactly parallel to that of the Fair 
Political Practices Commission. 
~he order in the Chaffey case was challenged on the grounds 
that it went beyond the authority of the commission, since the 
commission was not explicitly granted the power either to stay 
its order of dismissal or to order a temporary leave of absence. 
The court held that Section 11519 expressly conferred on all 
agencies covered by the APA the power to stay execution of penalties 
and to impose reasonable conditions of probation. 
The argument 
that the specific enumeration of powers in the statute creating 
the Commission on Professional Competence should prevail over the 
broad grant of powers of the more general APA was explicitly 
rejected by the court. 
The court noted that Section 11519 had 
been amended from an earlier statute which provided that an agency 
could stay a penalty and impose probation "where an agency has 
the power to make a probationary or conditional order." 
In amending 
the law, the Legislature specifically extended the power to suspend 
a penalty and to impose probationary conditions to all agencies 
covered by the APA, regardless of the language of their enabling 
statutes. 
This decision is consistent with the opinions of the 
Legislative Counsel's office and the Assembly Office of Research 
as to the purpose and effect of the amendment. 
72 Cal. App. 3d 
at 45, fn. 9. 
Finally, the court said, "the rigid restriction on 
the dispositional power of the commission as espoused by the 
district would not be in the public interest. 
The instant case 
is a classic example of the harmful result which would flow from 
an inflexible literal interpretation of the dispositional power 
granted the commission." 
at 460. 
Even without the explicit authority of Section 11519 as 
amended it is likely that the Commission could stay execution of 
its orders and put violators of the Act on probation subject to 
reasonable conditions. 
The State Bar has routinely placed attorneys 
on probation, staying execution of orders of suspension, under a 


Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
May 21, 1980 
Page Three 
statute which only appears to empower it to discipline members by 
"reproval, public or private," or by suspension from practice for 
a period not exceeding three years. 
Bus. & Prof. Code Section 
6077. E.g., Prantil v. State Bar, 23 Cal. 3d 243, 245 (1979). 
(Technically the State Bar only recommends to the Supreme Court 
on disciplinary matters. 
However, the Court adopts the reco~nendations 
in practically all cases unless the recommendation is "erroneous 
or unlawful." 
In essence, the Court reviews the recommendations 
of the board very much as any other court reviews the decisions 
of an administrative agency. 
Hawkins v. State Bar, 23 Cal. 3d 
622 (1979); Kitsis v. State Bar, 23 Cal. 3d 856 (1979). 
Cf. 
Brotsky v. State Bar, 57 Cal. 2d 287 (1962); Fish v. State Bar, 
214 Cal. 215 (1931).) 
Not only can the Commission place violators of the Act on 
probation subject to appropriate conditions, but it can probably 
issue a direct order that a violator do, or not do, things other 
than those specifically listed in Section 83116 of the Act without 
first placing him on probation. 
Courts have held that administrative 
agencies have implied power to fashion remedies to further the 
purposes of the law, even when their power to impose sanctions 
appears very limited. 
For example, in Pan American World Airways 
v. United States, 371 U. S. 391, 83 S.Ct. 476, 486 (1963), the 
Supreme Court upheld a CAB order of divestiture under a statute 
which only specifically gave the CAB power to issue a cease and 
desist order. 
The Court held that the power to order a company 
to cease and desist from anti-competitive practices necessarily 
implies the power to issue a divestiture order. 
The FTC, which 
is only given power by its statute to issue cease and desist 
orders, has consistently been upheld when it has issued orders 
requiring the giving of notice and other remedies. 
In Warner-
Lambert Co. v. FTC, 562 F. 2d 749, 756-757 (D.C. Cir. 1977), the 
court considered the claim of the company that the FTC could do 
no more than issue a "cease and desist" order as authorized by 
the statute, and could not issue a corrective order calling for 
affirmative action on the part of the company. 
The court held 
that the FTC could issue a corrective order, and could "shape 
remedies which go beyond the simple cease and desist order." 
The 
United States Supreme Court set forth its rationale for its repeated 
decisions that administrative agencies have an implicitly broad 
mandate to fashion administrative remedies in a case involving 
the NLRB: 
••• in the nature of things Congress could not catalog all 
the devices and strategems for circumventing the policies of 
the Act. 
Nor could it define the whole gamut of remedies to 
effectuate these policies in an infinite variety of specific 


Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
May 21, 1980 
Page Four 
situations. 
Congress met these difficulties by leaving the 
adaption of means to end to the empiric process of administration. 
The Act does not create rights for individuals which must be 
vindicated according to a rigid scheme of remedies. 
It 
entrusts to an expert agency the maintenance and promotion 
of industrial peace. 
Phelps Dodge Corp. v. NLRB, 1941, 
313 U. S. 177, 194, 61 S.Ct. 845, 
852. 
(2) 
Types of orders issued by other administrative agencies. 
Once having established that the FPPC can set conditions of 
probation, and can go beyond simple cease and desist or reporting 
orders, or the imposition of fines, the question arises as to 
just what kinds of orders are permissible. 
Neither Section 11519 
nor the Political Reform Act gives any guidance as to what orders 
are permissible or what conditions of probation are just and 
reasonable. 
Certainly the stay of execution of a penalty on the 
condition that the violator cease and desist from his unlawful 
actions, or that he file the reports or statements required by 
the Act is just and reasonable. 
Another type of just and reasonable 
condition of probation or permissible order is requiring the 
violator to give a specified notice to persons who have been, or 
may be, affected by his misconduct. 
In cases before the NLRB, 
parties found guilty of unfair labor practices are often required 
to give notice that they have been found guilty of unfair labor 
practices, and that they will not, in the future, engage in similar 
behavior. 
Parties may be required to give suitable publicity to 
their notice and, in the case of labor disputes, may be required 
to post notices on company or union bulletin boards, to mail 
notices to all members of a union or to all employees of companies 
involved, or to read notices to employees. 
E.g., NLRB v. Express 
Pub. Co., 312 U. S. 392, 438 61 S.Ct. 693, 701 (1941); NLRB v. 
Pennsylvania Greyhound Lines, 303 U. S. 261, 271, 58 S.Ct. 571, 
576 (1938); J.P. Stevens Co. v. NLRB, 417 F. 2d 533, 535 (5th 
Cir. 1969). 
Notices may be sent to former employees of a plant 
which has been permanently closed, Sturgis Newport Business Forms, 
Inc. v. NLRB, 563 F. 2d 1252, 1259 (5th Cir. 1977), or be posted 
for the benefit of prospective job applicants, NLRB v. Lummus 
Co., 210 F. 2d 377 (5th Cir. 1954). 
Violators have even been 
made to publish such a notice in the newspapers. 
NLRB v. Union 
National de Trabajores, 540 F. 2d 1, 11-12 (1st Cir. 1976). 
The NLRB cases are particularly useful in considering the 
powers of the FPPC, because they also deal with elections. 
In a 


Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
May 21, 1980 
Page Five 
typical case, notice is ordered to remedy misinformation or undue 
influence on the part of either the employer or the union with 
respect to a union election. 
In the case of the Political Reform 
Act, notice of past violations of the Act would serve basically 
the same purpose -- to correct misinformation or lack of information 
so that voters or supporters of a candidate would have the benefit 
of the information to which they are entitled under the Act for 
future elections. 
The NLRB is not the only agency which has required that 
notice be given by persons found to have violated the law. 
The 
FTC has used this remedy repeatedly in cases involving unfair or 
deceptive advertising. 
In Warner-Lambert Co. v. FTC, 562 F. 2d 
749 (D.C. Cir. 1977), the company was found to have engaged in 
nearly fifty years of false advertising that Listerine could 
prevent or alleviate the symptoms of colds and sore throats. 
Warner-Lambert was not only ordered to cease and desist from 
making these claims in any future advertisements, they were also 
ordered to make no future advertisements for Listerine unless the 
next $10 million-of advertising included in each advertisement a 
statement that nListerine will not help prevent colds or sore 
throats or lessen their severity.n 
Id. at 753. 
The court held 
that such corrective advertising was-appropriate, and that it did 
not violate the First Amendment. 
Id. at 758-59. 
Because a false 
impression may have been given in the past about the product, "To 
allow consumers to continue to buy the product on the strength of 
the impression built up by prior advertising -- an impression 
which is now known to be false -- would be unfair and deceptive. n 
Id. at 761. 
Many other instances of affirmative notice requirements may 
be found in the FTC cases. 
E.g., Credit Card Service Co. v. FTC, 
495 F. 2d 1004 (D.C. Cir. 1974) (notice in all advertising that 
credit card holders' liability is limited to $50); Simeon Mana~ement 
Corp. v. FTC, 579 F. 2d 1137 (9th Cir. 1978) (notice in advert~sements 
that weight reducing program uses drugs not approved by FDA and 
not more effective than simply controlling calorie intake). 
The 
FTC has ordered notice in cases which do not involve false or 
deceptive advertising. 
In Corning Glass Works v. FTC, 509 F. 2d 
293, 303 (7th Cir. 1975), Corning violated the law by requiring 
wholesalers to sign "fair trade" agreements with retailers, restricting 
the retail price of Corning products, in states where such agreements 
were illegal. 
Corning was required to mail notices to all retailers 
trading in their products in those states, informing them that 
the restrictions were illegal, and nencouragingn them to sell the 
products at any price they liked. 


Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
May 21, 1980 
Page Six 
Notice orders have also been approved by the California 
courts. 
In Pandol & Sons v. ALRB, 98 Cal. App. 3d 580 (1979), 
the court approved an order requiring the employer to mail, post, 
and read notices to employees, to provide a mailing list to the 
union, and to allow the union expanded access to the employer's 
premises. 
Id. at 585. 
In upholding the ALRB order, the court 
said that the agency did not need complete justification for all 
aspects of its order in the record of the particular case; it 
could draw on its experience and "insight" obtained in other 
cases in fashioning its remedy. 
Id. at 586-587. 
In general, the courts have held that administrative agencies 
have broad powers to tailor administrative reHledies to enforce 
their statutes. 
As the United States Supreme Court said, an 
order "should stand unless it can be shown that the order is a 
patent attempt to achieve ends other than those which can fairly 
be said to effectuate the policies of the act." 
Virginia Power & 
Electric Co. v. NLRB, 319 U. S. 533, 540, 63 S.Ct. 1214 (1974) 
1218. 
"A board must be given ample discretion in adapting remedy 
to violation." 
NLRB v. Chene 
Calif. Lumber Co., 327 U. S. 385, 
66 S.Ct. 553, 55 
(1 46), Call ornla courts have also emphasized 
that an agency must be given "relatively free reign" in adopting 
remedies. 
Pandol & Sons v. ALRB, 95 Ca. App. 3d at 588. 
The 
court has recognized that traditional remedies, such as fines, 
may be ineffective in some cases. 
For example, the court in J.P. 
Stevens Co. v. NLRB, 417 F. 2d 533, 538, fn. 9 (5th Cir. 1979r;--
pointed out that fines may be treated by employers as a "license 
fee for union busting," and upheld an order requiring extensive 
notice. 
The order was upheld even though one requirement -- that 
the employer actually read the notice to the employees -- might 
be "unnecessarily embarrassing and humiliating." 
Many other conditions of probation or types of orders are 
recognized by courts and administrative agencies, some of which 
might appropriately be employed by the Commission. 
For example, 
back pay, reinstatement in a job, and even an order that a person 
be hired if he was illegally refused employment are traditional 
remedies in labor cases. 
Phelps Dodge Corp. v. NLRB, 313 U. S. 
177, 61 S.Ct. 845 (1941). 
Back pay can be awarded for a job to 
which an employee was likely to have been promoted absent unfair 
labor practices. 
Butte View Farms v. ALRB, 95 Cal. App. 3d 961, 
969-70 (1979). 
Restitution to clients is often ordered by the State Bar in 
attorney disciplinary cases. 
E.g., Prantil v. State Bar, 23 Cal. 
3d 249, 245 (1979). 
Dentists found guilty of violations of their 
responsibilities may even be required to return patients' fees. 
Bus. and Prof. Code Section 1671(d). 
Attorneys are ordered by 


Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
May 21, 1980 
Page Seven 
the State Bar to make special reports concerning transactions 
with client funds. 
Yapp v. State Bar, 62 Cal. 2d 890 (1965). 
An 
attorney may be required to take and pass the professional respon-
sibility examination as a condition of probation. 
Prantil v. 
State Bar, 23 Cal. 3d 243, 247 (1979). 
Finally, if an administrative agency finds that the illegal 
conduct of a person has been so serious that none of the more 
usual remedies, such as cease and desist orders, reporting require-
ments, restitution, payment of fines, or notice will be effective, 
the agency may order even more drastic remedies. 
In cases in 
which the employer's unfair labor practices have made a fair 
election impossible, the union may be unable to win an election. 
The NLRB may nevertheless order the employer to recognize and 
bargain with the union. 
NLRB v. Gissel Packing Co., 89 S.Ct. 
1918, 1939-1940, 395 U. s. 575, 612-615 (1969). 
If an employer has succeeded in undermining a union's 
strength and destroying the laboratory conditions necessary 
for a fair election, he may see no need to violate a cease-
and-desist order by further unlawful activity. 
The damage 
will have been done, and perhaps the only fair way to ef-
fectuate employee rights is to re-establish the conditions 
as they existed before the employer's unlawful campaign. 
NLRB v. Gissel Packing Co., 89 
S.Ct. at 1939, 395 U. S. at 612. 
(3) 
Conditions of probation or other orders which the FPPC might 
utilize in appropriate cases. 
Looking at the cases discussed, and at the purposes of the 
Political Reform Act, it appears that the Commission has a very 
broad range of discretion in fashioning sanctions in individual 
cases. 
In ordering sanctions, however, the Commission should 
keep two general principles in mind. 
First, any sanction imQosed 
by the Commission, even one which is suspended, must be reasonably 
related to the circumstances of both the offense and the offender. 
If a sanction is arbitrary or capricious, or i7 clearly excessive, 
it can be overturned even if it is suspended.-
Second, the 
Commission should be mindful of the First Amendment implications 
!/ This issue was discussed in the memo from Barbara Milman 
to the Commission dated March 20, 1980. 
"The scope of the Com-
mission's discretion in imposing penalties under Section 83117." 


Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
May 21, 1980 
Page Eight 
of its orders. 
It should not impose sanctions which unreasonably 
burden First Amendment rights; on the other hand, it should not 
shrink from imposing appropriate sanctions out of an excess of 
caution. 
In considering whether a specific sanction violates the 
First Amendment the Commission should look to the principles 
enunciated in Buckley v. Valeo. 
So long as the sanctions are 
within the parameters set by Buckley, no constitutional problems 
should arise. 
For example, the Commission can require reasonable 
disclosure of contributions at different times or in different 
places than those specified in the Act, without violating the 
First Amendment rights of contributors. 
The Supreme Court's 
approval of disclosure of contributions was not limited to the 
specific methods of disclosure of the Act, but constituted general 
approval of all similar disclosure requirements. 
Similarly, 
orders requiring special reporting of expenditures which are 
otherwise reportable under the Act cause no constitutional problems, 
since such orders will not interfere with the First Amendment 
rights of contributors. 
Disclosure of expenditures was upheld as 
an auditing and enforcement device, as well as for its informational 
value to the public. 
Therefore remedial disclosure orders reasonably 
designed to further enforcement of the Act or to make the disclosure 
under the Act effective in particular cases are permissible, even 
if they go beyond the specific requirements of the Act. 
Orders which require notice by the violator that he has been 
found to have committed violations in the past, or that he intends 
to make certain types of reportable expenditures of funds raised 
from contributors, are also permissible under the First Amendment 
so long as they meet the ordinary standards of reasonableness 
relating to administrative sanctions. 
A candidate has no First 
Amendment right to withhold this type of information from the 
public. 
The fact that putting such information in a notice may 
deter people from contributing to or voting for the candidate 
does not violate the First Amendment. 
The basic purpose of the 
Act is to provide information to voters and contributors so they 
make informed decisions about candidates: one of the inevitable 
results of a law such as the Political Reform Act is that a candi-
date may lose support as a result of providing required information. 
Such a result does not violate the First Amendment. 
Within the framework of these general considerations, the 
following types of sanctions might be imposed by the Commission 
either under its implied powers to issue effective cease and 
desist orders, or as conditions of probation under Section 11519 
of the Administrative Procedure Act: 


Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
May 21, 1980 
Page Nine 
1. 
Reporting orders: 
The Commission could order a violator 
to comply with special reporting requirements, such as requiring 
special reports in addition to those required by the Act, requiring 
that supporting documents be filed along with reports, requiring 
a certification by a treasurer or the accountant who has prepared 
the reports, or requiring compliance within a fixed time period 
with an audit of the reports. 
Requirements such as these are 
analogous to some of the reporting requirements in attorney dis-
cipline cases. 
2. 
Notice orders: 
The Commission could order a violator to 
give notice that he has been found to have violated the Act, and 
that he failed to report, or misreported, specific items, listing 
them item-by-item, or by categories. 
The violator could be required 
to give the notice: 
(a) 
To past contributors who contributed in the period 
for which proper reporting was not made; 
(b) 
To past contributors who contributed after the 
reporting should have been made; 
(c) 
To persons to be solicited for future contributions 
for a specified period, measured either in terms of months, 
election campaigns, or the amount of money raised; 
(d) 
By pUblication in newspapers with circulation in 
the violator's jurisdiction. 
The notice orders would be similar to orders by the NLRB or the 
FTC in the cases discussed above. 
3. 
If the person was found to have committed a violation by 
failing to report expenditures for his own personal use, and/or 
if it were likely that he intended to pay a fine imposed by the 
Commission with money collected by fund raising, he might ~2 
required to notify prospective contributors: 
(a) 
That he intended to use at least some of the funds 
collected to pay the Commission fine, or for other personal 
purposes, if he did in fact have any such intention; 
(b) 
That he intended to pay his fine only from contribu-
tions made by contributors who specifically authorized in 
writing such use of their contributions, and that he might 
(if ordered to do so by the Commission) have to report which 
contributors had authorized him to use their contributions 
to pay his fine; 


Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
May 21, 1980 
Page Ten 
(c) 
That donations made to help him pay his fine might 
be construed as gifts rather than as campaign contributions, 
and, as gifts, would be reportable if they were 
$25 or more 
and would trigger the disqualification provisions of the 
Political Reform Act. 
Of course, before the Commission 
could make such an order it would have to make the threshold 
determination that contributions made for the purpose of 
paying a fine are gifts rather than contributions. 
This is 
a question which would require careful consideration by the 
Commission, and which might entail careful review of its 
past decisions. 
Orders such as these are most comparable to the orders in the 
NLRB and FTC cases. 
4. 
The violator might be required to take steps to insure 
that he would be able to comply with the Act in the future, such 
as obtaining a competent treasurer, studying the Act or taking a 
course in reporting under the Act, and be required to certify to 
the Commission what he had done as a condition of probation. 
This would be most similar to the orders of the State Bar and 
Supreme Court that attorneys found guilty of disciplinary violations 
take and pass the Professional Responsibility exam. 
5. 
The violator might be prohibited from using contributions 
to pay any fine imposed by the Commission. 
The theory behind 
this would be similar to that of the Gissel case, where the Commission 
would be determining that such an order is the only way to vindicate 
the rights of contributors who would not have contributed had 
they been provided in a timely fashion with the information he 
was required to file, but did not file, under the Act. 
6. 
Finally, most drastically, the violator might be required 
to offer to return campaign contributions to any contributor who 
made a contribution after the time that the reports should have 
been filed, on the theory that such persons might not have contributed 
had they known how the candidate used the money he raised in the 
past, or had they known who had contributed to his previous campaign. 
This remedy is most like the remedies of restitution in attorney 
cases, like the remedy that can be used against dentists, or like 
the remedy in Gissel. 
7. 
Any other remedy that the Commission can devise that 
appears to be appropriate in a particular case. 
The only restrictions 
on the remedies that can be used are that, if imposed as a condition 
of probation, they be "just and reasonable in the light of the 


Commissioners Houston, Lapan, 
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn 
May 21, 1980 
Page Eleven 
findings and decision," 
Government Code Section 11519, and that 
in any case they not be unrelated to the violations of which he 
was found guilty. 
NLRB v. Express Pub. Co., 312 U. S. 392, 433, 
61 S.Ct. 693, 698 (1941). 
BM:p1h

sections.question_synthetic: What sanctions and remedial powers does the FPPC have beyond those explicitly listed in Section 83116 of the Political Reform Act, particularly regarding stay of execution, probationary conditions, and other corrective orders?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC has broad authority under Government Code Section 11519 and the Administrative Procedure Act to stay execution of penalties, impose reasonable probationary conditions, and fashion additional remedies beyond cease-and-desist orders and fines, including notice requirements and corrective orders analogous to those used by the NLRB and FTC.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is an internal FPPC memorandum analyzing legal authority rather than a traditional advice letter responding to an external request. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present, but analytical structure clearly addresses a legal question and provides authoritative answer. Analysis includes extensive case law citations from Chaffey, NLRB, FTC, and other administrative law precedent.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What sanctions and remedial powers does the FPPC have beyond those explicitly listed in Section 83116 of the Political Reform Act, particularly regarding stay of execution, probationary conditions, and other corrective orders?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC has broad authority under Government Code Section 11519 and the Administrative Procedure Act to stay execution of penalties, impose reasonable probationary conditions, and fashion additional remedies beyond cease-and-desist orders and fines, including notice requirements and corrective orders analogous to those used by the NLRB and FTC.

Section 83116 also provides that the Commission shall have all the powers granted by Chapter 5 of the Administrative Procedure Act, including Government Code Section 11519. Section 11519 provides: (b) A stay of execution may be included in the decision or if not included therein may be granted by the agency at any time before the decision becomes effective. The stay of execution provided herein may be accompanied by an express condition that respondent comply with specified terms of probation~ provided, however, that the terms of probation shall be just and reasonable in the light of the findings and decision. Combining the language of the Act with that of Section 11519, the Commission is empowered to stay any penalty imposed under the Act, and to impose any conditions of probation which are just and reasonable as a condition of the stay. A case involving almost an identical statutory situation was decided in 1977 in Governing Board of the Chaffey Union High School District v. Comm'n. on Professional Competence, 72 Cal· Commissioners Houston, Lapan, McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn May 21, 1980 Page Two App. 3d 447 (1977). In that case the Commission on Professional Competence ordered that a teacher be permanently dismissed, and then ordered the dismissal permanently stayed subject to the condition that the teacher take a two year leave of absence without pay and that he not request attorney's fees. The Commission on Professional Compentence was acting under a statute which gave it authority only to dismiss an employee or to order that the employee not be dismissed. The Commission was not given any other remedial powers by the statute. However, the statute did provide that its proceedings would be covered by the APA, and that it would have "all the power granted to an agency by the APA." Thus the statutory authority of the Commission on Professional Competence in the Chaffey case was almost exactly parallel to that of the Fair Political Practices Commission. ~he order in the Chaffey case was challenged on the grounds that it went beyond the authority of the commission, since the commission was not explicitly granted the power either to stay its order of dismissal or to order a temporary leave of absence. The court held that Section 11519 expressly conferred on all agencies covered by the APA the power to stay execution of penalties and to impose reasonable conditions of probation. embedding.summary: This memorandum to commissioners addresses the FPPC's power to impose administrative sanctions beyond those explicitly listed in Section 83116, concluding that the Commission can stay penalties, impose probation conditions, and issue corrective orders consistent with APA provisions and precedent from other administrative agencies.
